text: Which among the following is most common in alkyl halides?
explanation:
Alkyl halides have a polar C—X bond (\(\overset{\delta+}{\text{C}}\text{—}\overset{\delta-}{\text{X}}\)). The carbon is electron-deficient and susceptible to attack by **nucleophiles** (electron-rich species). Alkyl halides readily undergo **nucleophilic substitution** reactions:

- **\(S_N2\)**: Bimolecular, concerted backside attack by nucleophile
- **\(S_N1\)**: Unimolecular, via carbocation intermediate

\[\overset{\delta+}{\text{R}}\text{—}\overset{\delta-}{\text{X}} + \text{Nu}^\ominus \rightarrow \text{R—Nu} + \text{X}^-\]
name: Questions Item 1

text: The C—Cl bond in chlorobenzene compared to the C—Cl bond in methyl chloride is
explanation:
In chlorobenzene, the lone pairs of the Cl atom are **delocalised into the benzene ring** through the **+R effect**:

\[\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{—Cl} \leftrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5^{\delta-}\text{...Cl}^{\delta+}\]

This gives the C—Cl bond a **partial double bond character**, which makes it:
- **Shorter** than in methyl chloride (more s-character, stronger bond)
- **Stronger** (higher bond dissociation energy)

In CH₃Cl, no such delocalisation occurs, so the C—Cl is a pure single bond — longer and weaker.
name: Questions Item 2

text: The C—Cl bond in chlorobenzene compared to the C—Cl bond in methyl chloride is
explanation: Due to the **+R effect** of the Cl atom in chlorobenzene, the lone pairs of Cl are delocalised into the benzene ring, giving the C—Cl bond **partial double bond character**. This makes the C—Cl bond in chlorobenzene **shorter and stronger** compared to the pure single bond C—Cl in methyl chloride (CH₃Cl), where no such resonance is possible.
name: Questions Item 3

text:
Which among the following statements is true about the reactivity of haloarenes towards nucleophilic substitution reactions?

1. Haloarenes are very reactive towards nucleophilic substitution.
2. In haloarenes, the C—X bond acquires partial double bond character.
3. The C—X bond in haloarenes is longer than the C—X bond in haloalkanes.
4. In haloarenes, the phenyl cation formed by self-ionisation is stable.
explanation:
Due to the **+R effect** of the halogen atom X in haloarenes, the C—X bond acquires **partial double bond character** through delocalisation of the lone pair into the ring:

\[\text{Ar—X} \leftrightarrow ^-\text{Ar...X}^+\]

This partial double bond character makes the C—X bond **shorter** (not longer) than in haloalkanes and makes nucleophilic substitution **difficult** (not easy) — making statements 1, 3, and 4 incorrect.

Only **Statement 2** is correct.
name: Questions Item 4

text:
The increasing order of densities of the following alkyl halides is:

I. \(n\text{-C}_3\text{H}_7\text{Br}\)  II. \(n\text{-C}_3\text{H}_7\text{Cl}\)  III. \(n\text{-C}_3\text{H}_7\text{I}\)
explanation:
The density of alkyl halides increases with the **atomic mass of the halogen**:

| Compound | Halogen | Density |
|---|---|---|
| \(n\text{-C}_3\text{H}_7\text{Cl}\) | Cl (35.5) | 0.89 g/mL |
| \(n\text{-C}_3\text{H}_7\text{Br}\) | Br (80) | 1.335 g/mL |
| \(n\text{-C}_3\text{H}_7\text{I}\) | I (127) | 1.747 g/mL |

Increasing order: **II < I < III** (\(n\text{-C}_3\text{H}_7\text{Cl} < n\text{-C}_3\text{H}_7\text{Br} < n\text{-C}_3\text{H}_7\text{I}\))
name: Questions Item 5

text: Which among the following compounds is a gem-dihalide?
explanation:
A **gem-dihalide** (geminal dihalide) has **two halogen atoms on the same carbon atom**.

- **Ethylidene chloride** (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CHCl}_2\)): 2 Cl atoms on C2 → **gem-dihalide** ✓
- Ethylene dichloride (\(\text{ClCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}\)): Cl on different carbons → vicinal dihalide ✗
- Methyl chloride (\(\text{CH}_3\text{Cl}\)): only one Cl → monohalide ✗
- Benzyl chloride (\(\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}\)): only one Cl ✗
name: Questions Item 6

text: For the reaction \(\text{H}_2\text{C=CH}_2 + \text{Br}_2(aq) \xrightarrow{\text{NaCl}} \text{Products}\), which of the following is NOT a possible product?
explanation:
The reaction proceeds via **electrophilic addition**. Br₂ provides the electrophile Br⁺ which attacks the C=C to form a bromonium ion (or carbocation). The carbocation is then attacked by the available nucleophiles in solution:

- \(\text{Br}^-\) → gives **\(\text{BrCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{Br}\)** ✓
- \(\text{Cl}^-\) (from NaCl) → gives **\(\text{BrCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}\)** ✓
- \(\text{OH}^-\)/\(\text{H}_2\text{O}\) → gives **\(\text{BrCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{OH}\)** ✓

However, **\(\text{ClCH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}\)** is NOT possible — Cl₂ is not the electrophile here, so the initial attack is always by Br⁺. The product must have at least one Br. Two-Cl product cannot form.
name: Questions Item 7

text: Which one of the following properties has the same value for \(\text{H}_2\) and \(\text{D}_2\)?
explanation:
\(\text{H}_2\) and \(\text{D}_2\) are **isotopes** — same number of protons (Z = 1) but different mass number (H has mass 1; D has mass 2).

**Bond length** depends on the **electrostatic interaction** between the electron cloud and the nucleus, which is determined by the atomic number (Z) and the electron configuration — **not by the mass**. Since H and D have the same Z and electron arrangement, the H—H and D—D bond lengths are **identical**.

Density and melting point differ (D₂ is heavier); bond dissociation enthalpy also differs slightly due to zero-point energy differences.
name: Questions Item 8

text:
Arrange the following organic halides in the correct order of reactivity towards \(S_N2\) displacement:

(P) \((\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C(Br)CH}_2\text{CH}_3\)  (Q) \(\text{BrCH}_2(\text{CH}_2)_3\text{CH}_3\)  (R) \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH(Br)(CH}_2)_2\text{CH}_3\)
explanation:
In \(S_N2\) reactions, the rate is inversely proportional to **steric hindrance** at the carbon bearing the leaving group:

- **(P)** \((\text{CH}_3)_2\text{C(Br)CH}_2\text{CH}_3\): **3° alkyl halide** — highly hindered → slowest \(S_N2\)
- **(R)** \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH(Br)(CH}_2)_2\text{CH}_3\): **2° alkyl halide** — moderately hindered
- **(Q)** \(\text{BrCH}_2(\text{CH}_2)_3\text{CH}_3\): **1° alkyl halide** — least hindered → fastest \(S_N2\)

\[\text{Rate: } S_N2 \propto \frac{1}{\text{steric hindrance}} \Rightarrow 1° > 2° > 3°\]

Order: **Q > R > P**
name: Questions Item 9

text:
Which of the following statements about chlorobenzene are correct?

A. The C—Cl bond in chlorobenzene is shorter than in chloromethane.
B. It is difficult to replace chlorine from chlorobenzene than from benzyl chloride.
C. The C—Cl bond in chlorobenzene has some double bond character.
D. Chlorobenzene on chlorination gives m-dichlorobenzene.
explanation:
**Statement A — Correct:** The +R effect gives the C—Cl bond in chlorobenzene partial double bond character → **shorter** than in CH₃Cl.

**Statement B — Correct:** The partial double bond character makes C—Cl in chlorobenzene stronger and harder to break → **more difficult** to replace Cl than in benzyl chloride (where Cl is on a sp³ carbon with no resonance).

**Statement C — Correct:** Due to delocalisation of Cl's lone pair into the ring → **double bond character** ✓

**Statement D — Incorrect:** Cl is an **ortho/para-directing** group (activating via +M effect), so chlorobenzene on chlorination gives **o- and p-dichlorobenzene**, NOT m-dichlorobenzene.

Correct statements: **A, B, C**.
name: Questions Item 10

text:
What are X, Y and Z in the following reactions?

Reaction 1: \((\text{H}_3\text{C})_3\text{CONa}^{\oplus\ominus} + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Br} \rightarrow X + \text{NaBr}\)

Reaction 2: \((\text{H}_3\text{C})_3\text{CBr} + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{ONa}^{\oplus\ominus} \rightarrow Y + Z\)
explanation:
These are **Williamson synthesis** reactions. The product depends on the type of halide and alkoxide:

**Reaction 1:** 3° alkoxide ion \(((\text{H}_3\text{C})_3\text{CO}^-)\) + **1° halide** (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Br}\)) → \(S_N2\) gives **ether**:
\[(\text{H}_3\text{C})_3\text{CONa} + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{Br} \rightarrow \underbrace{(\text{H}_3\text{C})_3\text{COCH}_2\text{CH}_3}_{X} + \text{NaBr}\]

**Reaction 2:** **3° halide** \(((\text{H}_3\text{C})_3\text{CBr})\) + 1° alkoxide ion (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{O}^-\)) → The 3° halide cannot undergo \(S_N2\); instead **elimination (E2)** occurs giving **alkene + alcohol**:
\[(\text{H}_3\text{C})_3\text{CBr} + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{ONa} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{C(=CH}_2)\text{CH}_3}_{Y} + \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{OH}}_{Z}\]
name: Questions Item 11

text:
Assertion (A): Propene on addition with HBr in the presence of peroxide gives 1-bromopropane as the major product.

Reason (R): 1-bromopropane is the major product because it is formed through a stable carbocation.
options: (A) and (R) are correct; (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
(A) and (R) are correct but (R) is not the correct explanation of (A)
(A) is correct but (R) is not correct
(A) is not correct but (R) is correct
explanation:
**Assertion (A) — Correct:** In the presence of **peroxide**, HBr addition to propene follows **anti-Markovnikov's rule** (free radical mechanism), giving **1-bromopropane** as the major product.

**Reason (R) — Incorrect:** The reason given is wrong because:
- In **free radical mechanism** (with peroxide), the regioselectivity is determined by the **stability of the radical intermediate**, NOT a carbocation
- The **secondary radical** (\(\text{CH}_3\overset{•}{\text{CH}}\text{CH}_3\)) is more stable than the primary radical (\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\overset{•}{\text{CH}}_2\))
- But the Br• adds to the terminal carbon (less substituted) → gives 1-bromopropane via the more stable secondary radical

The mechanism involves **radicals, not carbocations**.

**(A) is correct but (R) is not correct.**
name: Questions Item 12

text: Identify the halogen exchange reaction from the following.
explanation:
**Finkelstein reaction** involves the **exchange of one halogen for another** in an alkyl halide. It is an \(S_N2\) reaction:

\[\text{R—X} + \text{NaY} \xrightarrow{\text{acetone}} \text{R—Y} + \text{NaX}\]

Commonly used to convert alkyl chlorides or bromides to alkyl iodides (since NaI is soluble in acetone, while NaCl/NaBr are not — equilibrium shifts right).

- **Sandmeyer reaction**: converts diazonium salts to aryl halides
- **Fittig reaction**: coupling of two aryl halides using Na
- **Wurtz-Fittig**: coupling of aryl + alkyl halides using Na
name: Questions Item 13

text: The rate of dehydrohalogenation of which one among the following is least?
explanation:
Rate of dehydrohalogenation (β-elimination) depends on two factors:

**1. Degree of alkyl halide:** \(3° > 2° > 1°\)

**2. Reactivity of leaving group:** \(\text{I} > \text{Br} > \text{Cl} > \text{F}\)

Among options (a), (b), (c): all are **1° (primary)** alkyl halides with the same chain but different halogens. Since I is the best leaving group and Cl is the worst:

\[\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{I} > \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{Br} > \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}\]

The rate of dehydrohalogenation is **least** for **\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{Cl}\)** (1° + Cl = slowest).
name: Questions Item 14

text: Propyne reacts with HBr to form Z. The compound Z is
explanation:
Propyne undergoes **electrophilic addition** of HBr. The addition follows Markovnikov's rule at each step:

**Step 1:** HBr adds to the triple bond:
\[\text{CH}_3\text{—C≡CH} + \text{HBr} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{—C(Br)=CH}_2\]

(Br adds to the more substituted carbon — Markovnikov)

**Step 2:** Second HBr adds:
\[\text{CH}_3\text{—C(Br)=CH}_2 + \text{HBr} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{—CBr}_2\text{—CH}_3}_{\text{2,2-dibromopropane}}\]

Product Z = **\(\text{CH}_3\text{CBr}_2\text{CH}_3\)** (2,2-dibromopropane)
name: Questions Item 15

text: \(S_N2\) reaction involving inversion of configuration takes place with an optically active compound Z. The compound Z is
explanation:
For \(S_N2\) with **inversion of configuration**, two requirements must be met:

1. The compound must be **optically active** → must have a **chiral centre** (carbon bonded to 4 different groups)
2. The carbon must be accessible for backside attack (not too hindered)

Checking options:
- \(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{X}\): no chiral centre ✗
- \((\text{CH}_3)_2\text{CHX}\): 2 identical CH₃ groups → **no chiral centre** ✗
- **\(\text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH(CH}_3)\text{X}\)**: C2 has H, CH₃, C₂H₅, and X → **4 different groups → chiral → optically active** ✓ (2° halide, accessible for \(S_N2\))
- \((\text{CH}_3)_3\text{CX}\): 3° → \(S_N2\) doesn't occur ✗
name: Questions Item 16

text: Which one of the following has the highest dipole moment?
explanation:
Dipole moment (\(\mu\)) arises when bond dipoles do not cancel:

- **cis-but-2-ene**: Both CH₃ groups are on the same side, but C—CH₃ dipoles nearly cancel → \(\mu = 0.33\) D (small)
- **trans-1,2-dichloroethene**: The two C—Cl dipoles point in opposite directions → they **cancel exactly** → \(\mu = 0\) D
- **cis-1,2-dichloroethene**: Both C—Cl dipoles point in the **same direction** (same side of C=C) → they **add up** → \(\mu = 1.89\) D (**highest**) ✓
- **trans-but-2-ene**: CH₃ groups on opposite sides, dipoles cancel → \(\mu = 0\) D

**cis-1,2-dichloroethene** has the highest dipole moment.
name: Questions Item 17
